title: TPU v1 · Power-On and Initialization Sequence for a Host-Driven Coprocessor
oneLine: This reset and initialization case shows how a coprocessor that cannot boot itself is brought up: power-on reset, host configuration over PCIe, memory-interface initialization and weight loading, then READY before commands are accepted. The sequence must be repeatable and debuggable.

legend.name: ① Power-on reset (POR)
legend.desc: After power is applied, wait for rails and clocks—including PLL lock—to stabilize, then force all registers and state machines into a known state. TPU v1 did not publish a proprietary reset mechanism; this follows standard data-center ASIC practice.

legend.name: ② Host configuration over PCIe
legend.desc: TPU is a coprocessor and does not self-boot. The host enumerates it over PCIe 3.0, writes configuration registers, and establishes control and command channels, making host ownership explicit.

legend.name: ③ Memory-interface initialization
legend.desc: Before 8 GiB of DDR3 can deliver ~30 GB/s, its PHY must train and calibrate timing. This is a prerequisite for supplying weights reliably.

legend.name: ④ Load weights into DDR3
legend.desc: The host places model weights in 8 GiB of DDR3 before computation, preparing the weight-stationary dataflow in which weights are reused after loading.

legend.name: ⑤ READY permits commands
legend.desc: Only after all earlier stages pass does the device enter READY. The host may then issue roughly a dozen CISC commands and begin full-rate inference.

steps.cap: ① Power-on reset: wait for stable rails and clocks, then put the entire chip in a known initial state.
② The host enumerates and configures TPU over PCIe, establishing control and command channels; the coprocessor does not self-start.
③ Initialize the memory interface by training and calibrating the DDR3 PHY, enabling the ~30 GB/s weight channel.
④ Load model weights from the host into 8 GiB of off-chip DDR3 for weight-stationary execution.
⑤ Accept CISC commands only in READY; the entire sequence must be repeatable, observable, and restartable after failure.

before: Establish the system role: TPU v1 is a PCIe coprocessor that does not self-boot. The startup owner and ordering must be explicit. Published facts include host-driven operation, PCIe 3.0, about a dozen CISC commands, and 8 GiB DDR3 at ~30 GB/s.

Decisions to make: Reset hierarchy and scope, synchronous versus asynchronous release, startup ordering and dependencies, host/device handshakes and state machines, memory training, and observability from POR to READY.
Required inputs: Rail and clock sequencing, PLL lock time, initialization procedures for PCIe and DDR3 IP, reset needs of the core/PCIe/DDR3 domains in §3.8, and the driver configuration flow.
Hard requirements: Determinism—every boot reaches the same known state—and debuggability—any stuck stage can be identified and reset cleanly. These are prerequisites for production and operation.

Disclosure boundary: Public v1 material contains little proprietary reset detail. The sequence is standard data-center inference-ASIC practice, using host-driven PCIe, DDR3, and CISC only as disclosed context.
during: Reset, configure, then release: POR must establish known hardware before host configuration, and READY must follow configuration. Advancing without confirmation hides faults until runtime.

Make command ownership explicit: A non-self-booting coprocessor needs clear host↔device handshakes, configuration registers, command/response channels, and READY/BUSY status so software always knows the current stage.
Warm up the memory interface: DDR3 cannot immediately sustain ~30 GB/s. PHY training and calibration align timing and must finish before weights are loaded, or transfers may fail silently.

Align weight loading with dataflow: Weight-stationary execution needs weights resident first. Populate DDR3 during initialization and enforce “load before compute” so the array never waits for initial data.
Design a deterministic sequence: Fix startup ordering, define timeouts and retries, and avoid uninitialized dependencies so every reset converges to the same state.

Build observability and re-entrant reset: Expose status and handshakes at every stage and allow a clean return to POR from any failure. These are standard practices; exact v1 mechanisms were not published.

result.label: Initialization sequence
result.value: POR → PCIe config → memory init → load weights → READY

result.label: Release condition
result.value: READY before ~a dozen CISC commands are accepted

after: Verify deterministic startup: Repeat power-on and reset across many cycles, confirming the same known state and READY timing. Investigate uninitialized values or sequencing dependencies if behavior varies.

Stress memory initialization: Confirm DDR3 PHY training across voltage and temperature corners at target bandwidth. Failures need explicit diagnostics and retry paths, never silent degraded operation.

Propagate constraints: Give physical implementation reset-tree, power-order, and rail-timing requirements; give drivers the host configuration protocol and status definitions; give verification an end-to-end cold-start-to-READY test.
Preserve observability and re-entry: Add status registers and counters for every stage and support a clean return to POR from any point, essential for field diagnosis of startup failures.
Document facts versus practice: Distinguish published host-driven/PCIe/DDR3/CISC facts from inferred reset trees, state machines, and training flows based on standard inference-ASIC engineering.
source: In-Datacenter Performance Analysis of a TPU, Jouppi et al., ISCA 2017 (host-driven operation, PCIe, DDR3, and CISC are published; reset/initialization sequencing, state machines, and memory training are standard data-center inference-ASIC practice.)
